content: 3 distinct auth schemes coexist: (1) Terminal JWT (jose HS256) via requireTerminalAuth for device APIs, (2) Supabase Auth cookies via createClient().auth.getUser() for portal APIs, (3) X-Merchant-Key header for merchant sync. No central auth dispatcher.

TerminalJWTPayload has NO exp claim — createTerminalJWT only sets IssuedAt. The refresh route uses clockTolerance: 999_999_999 — effectively perpetual tokens. Architectural: token lifetime policy is absent from the contract.

Inconsistency: requireTerminalAuth does NOT check terminal.status='inactive'. /api/device/token/refresh and /api/payment/approve do this check independently. Status validation duplicated in route handlers instead of central middleware.

app/api/device/heartbeat re-reads Authorization header AFTER requireTerminalAuth to extract exp — duplicates parsing. Same in refresh route which re-implements jwtVerify with custom clockTolerance instead of using verifyTerminalJWT.
middleware.ts at root does not exist. Auth applied per-route only. No edge-level filtering — every route handler must remember to call requireTerminalAuth or auth.getUser().

terminals/[id]/revoke nulls access_token in DB but JWT is signature-validated only — revocation has no effect on JWT verification path. Architectural disconnect between stored access_token and stateless JWT.
app/api/terminals/route.ts mixes auth: POST uses cookie auth, GET uses requireTerminalAuth — same URL, different schemes by method. Confusing for clients/maintainers.
Type weakness: verifyTerminalJWT casts via `as unknown as TerminalJWTPayload` — no runtime validation (zod) of claims. Untrusted decoded JWT trusted as typed.
